name: Kafka — producer write, 3x async replication (Kreps 2014)
notes: Single producer thread writing 100-byte records to a 3-broker Kafka cluster (6 partitions), 3x ASYNCHRONOUS replication — the realistic production durability config, from the SAME Kreps 2014 benchmark as the no-replication entry. Modeled identically — SDA models the aggregate cluster throughput, not the replication factor, so both are the same cluster under a different durability config (the analogue of TechEmpower single/multi and Redis SET/LPUSH: one architecture, two configs, one shared tunable). The shared tunable stream.kafka:throughput makes the fit over-determined; the leave-one-out (fit on the no-replication point, predict this one) is a genuine out-of-sample test. Same scope and config-sensitivity caveats as the no-replication entry — this validates the write CEILING, not the queue-backlog behaviour, and does not flip the act-as-a-queue family.

sources.note: Jay Kreps, 'Benchmarking Apache Kafka: 2 Million Writes Per Second (On Three Cheap Machines)', LinkedIn Engineering, 2014-04-27. Single producer thread, 3x asynchronous replication: 786,980 records/sec (75.1 MB/sec). Same hardware / 100-byte records / six partitions as the no-replication run.

tunables.note: The SAME free variable as the no-replication entry (selector stream.kafka, key throughput) — one shared Kafka aggregate throughput. Sharing it across the two replication configs is what over-determines the fit and makes the leave-one-out a genuine out-of-sample test; the small residual is the async-replication overhead SDA does not separately model.

groundTruth.note: Single producer thread, 3x async replication: 786,980 records/sec (Kreps 2014). Marginally below the no-replication 821,557 (async replication is cheap); SDA models both with ONE aggregate throughput, so the ~4% gap is the irreducible residual a single capacity value cannot capture.
